turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has our opinion this morning in case number 16-74, Advocate Health Care Network versus Stapleton in the consolidated cases.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The Employee Retirement Income Security Act, ERISA for short, requires private employers offering pension plans to follow various rules designed to ensure plan's solvency and to protect plan participants. But ERISA exempts church plans from those rules. The question we decide in this case has to do with what counts as a church plan. More specifically, the question is whether a plan maintained by a church-affiliated organization for its employees qualifies as a church plan, even when it is not originally established by a church. The petitioners here are three church-affiliated nonprofits that run hospitals. They established pension plans for their employees. The respondents are those hospital employees. They filed class actions alleging that the hospital's plans do not fall within ERISA's church plan exemption because they were not established by a church. So the employees say the plans have to comply with all of ERISA's many requirements. The courts below agreed with the employees, but we do not. And to be frank, this is the kind of case where you have to stare at the statutory language to understand why. But here is a stab and an explanation. Suppose Congress described some group of benefit plans, let's call them “Class A plans” and said, those plans are exempt from ERISA. And then several years later, Congress said, “From here on out, Class A plans will include Class C plans. Now, the question is, whether Class C plans are also exempt from ERISA. And the answer is yes. If A is exempt and A includes C, then C is exempt too. You might say that's elementary logic, and that's exactly what Congress did here. From the beginning ERISA said that “Plans established and maintained by a church are exempt from ERISA,” and then Congress said “Plans established and maintained by a church include plans maintained by certain church-affiliated organizations.” And then the question is, are all plans meeting that latter description exempt, regardless of whether they were established by a church? And the answer is yes, because again, if A is exempt and A includes C, then C is exempt too. We have some other arguments relating to both the text and Congress' purpose in enacting it, but that is really the heart of the matter. Given how the statute reads, the employees have not established that the hospital's benefit plans must comply with ERISA. Accordingly, we reverse the judgments below. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of these cases.
